meta.title: Three.js Performance Optimization for SOTY Event Horizon
meta.author: Cleanlystudio
meta.description: Comprehensive Three.js-specific performance database for high-end WebGL experiences

minimize_children.description: Three.js traverses the entire scene graph every frame for matrix updates. Fewer objects = faster traversal.
rules: Flatten the scene graph when possible — don't nest objects unnecessarily
Use InstancedMesh instead of many individual meshes for repeated geometry
Group static objects and merge their geometry
Remove objects from the scene when they're not visible (not just set visible: false)
minimize_children.cost_per_object: ~0.01-0.05ms per Object3D for matrix update + frustum check. At 1000 objects = 10-50ms overhead.
minimize_children.target: Keep scene graph under 500 objects for consistent 60fps on mid-range hardware

frustum_culling.description: Three.js automatically skips rendering objects outside the camera frustum (enabled by default)
frustum_culling.when_to_disable: For objects that are always visible (fullscreen quads, background spheres, the black hole itself)
frustum_culling.custom_bounding: Set custom bounding spheres for large/complex objects: mesh.geometry.boundingSphere = new THREE.Sphere(center, radius)
frustum_culling.note: Frustum culling has a per-object CPU cost. For very large particle systems, it may be cheaper to disable culling and let the GPU handle it.

matrix_auto_update.description: Every frame, Three.js recalculates the world matrix of every object. For static objects, this is wasted work.
matrix_auto_update.manual_update: Call object.updateMatrix() only when position/rotation/scale changes
matrix_auto_update.apply_to: Background meshes, static scene elements, any object that doesn't move
matrix_auto_update.savings: Significant for scenes with many static objects — eliminates matrix multiplication per object per frame

object_pooling.description: Pre-create a pool of objects at initialization. Enable/disable them instead of creating/destroying.

pattern.init: Create N objects, set all to visible: false. Store in pool array.
pattern.acquire: Find first invisible object, set visible: true, configure, return reference.
pattern.release: Set visible: false, reset properties, return to pool.

object_pooling.ideal_for: Particle effects, temporary visual effects, debris, any objects that appear/disappear frequently
object_pooling.advantage: Zero GC pressure. No memory allocation during gameplay. Predictable performance.
object_pooling.three_js_specific: Don't remove/add to scene — just toggle .visible. Adding/removing triggers scene graph rebuild.

ShaderMaterial.description: Raw GLSL shader. Fastest execution. No built-in material overhead.
ShaderMaterial.overhead: Minimal — only your uniforms, no automatic uniform generation
ShaderMaterial.use_when: Maximum performance required. Custom effects. Black hole rendering. Post-processing.
ShaderMaterial.drawback: Must implement everything manually (lighting, shadows, fog, etc.)

RawShaderMaterial.description: Like ShaderMaterial but doesn't prepend Three.js standard headers
RawShaderMaterial.overhead: Absolute minimum — full control over every line of GLSL
RawShaderMaterial.use_when: GPGPU shaders, post-processing passes, any shader where Three.js prefixes are unwanted

MeshStandardMaterial.description: PBR material with environment mapping, roughness, metalness
MeshStandardMaterial.overhead: High — complex shader with many branches and texture lookups
MeshStandardMaterial.use_when: Physically accurate rendering of solid objects. Not needed for Event Horizon's abstract effects.

material_reuse.description: Reuse the same material instance across multiple meshes. Each unique material = potential draw call break.
material_reuse.rule: If two meshes look the same, they MUST share the same material instance
material_reuse.good: const sharedMat = new THREE.ShaderMaterial(...); meshes.forEach(m => m.material = sharedMat)
material_reuse.savings: Reduces shader program switches and uniform uploads

on_before_compile.description: Hook into built-in material shader compilation to inject custom code
on_before_compile.use_case: Add custom vertex displacement to a standard material without writing a full custom shader
on_before_compile.caveat: Changes are per-material instance. The modified shader is cached — changing onBeforeCompile after first render has no effect unless material.needsUpdate = true

needs_update.description: material.needsUpdate = true forces shader recompilation. VERY expensive.
needs_update.rule: Set to false after initial setup. Only set to true if shader #defines change.
needs_update.what_doesnt_need_it: Changing uniform values (including textures). Changing material.opacity, color, etc.
needs_update.what_needs_it: Changing material.defines, changing flatShading, changing vertexColors, switching map on/off

transparent_objects.description: Transparent materials require back-to-front sorting and disable early-z rejection
transparent_objects.performance_impact: 2-3x more expensive than opaque rendering due to blending + sort
optimization: Use alpha test (alphaTest: 0.5) instead of transparency when possible — allows early-z
Use premultiplied alpha for cleaner blending: material.premultipliedAlpha = true
For particles: use additive blending (blending: THREE.AdditiveBlending) — no sorting needed because order doesn't matter
Sort transparent objects manually if Three.js auto-sort is insufficient

buffer_geometry_only.description: Always use BufferGeometry. The old Geometry class was removed in r125.
buffer_geometry_only.rule: Every geometry must be a BufferGeometry with typed array attributes

indexed_geometry.description: Share vertices between triangles using an index buffer
indexed_geometry.benefit: Reduces vertex count by 30-60% for typical meshes. Less memory, fewer vertex shader invocations.
indexed_geometry.when_to_skip: Flat-shaded meshes where vertices CAN'T be shared (different normals per face)

interleaved_buffers.description: Store all vertex attributes (position, normal, uv) in a single interleaved buffer for better GPU cache coherency
interleaved_buffers.benefit: When the vertex shader reads position, normal, and UV, they're all in the same cache line
interleaved_buffers.savings: 5-15% vertex processing speedup on cache-sensitive GPUs (mobile)

instanced_buffer_geometry.description: Draw thousands of instances of the same geometry with per-instance attributes

implementation.setup: new THREE.InstancedBufferGeometry() with InstancedBufferAttribute for per-instance data
implementation.per_instance_data: Position (vec3), scale (float), color (vec3), rotation (quaternion), velocity (vec3), age (float)
implementation.update: instancedMesh.instanceMatrix.needsUpdate = true after modifying matrix data

instanced_buffer_geometry.ideal_for: Particle systems (10k-1M particles), star fields, debris, any repeated geometry
instanced_buffer_geometry.performance: 10,000 particles = 1 draw call. CPU cost is only the matrix/attribute update, not per-particle draw call overhead.
instanced_buffer_geometry.event_horizon_use: Star field (50k+ stars), accretion disk particles (10k+), debris particles

merge_static_geometry.description: Combine multiple static meshes into a single geometry to reduce draw calls
merge_static_geometry.caveat: Merged geometry can't be individually transformed or have different materials
merge_static_geometry.ideal_for: Background star meshes, static environment elements

lod_levels_of_detail.description: Show simpler geometry for distant objects
lod_levels_of_detail.implementation: THREE.LOD object with addLevel(mesh, distance) for each detail level
lod_levels_of_detail.event_horizon_relevance: Low — the scene is mostly particles and shaders, not polygon meshes. But useful if any geometric objects are present.

pixel_ratio.description: Control the render resolution relative to CSS pixels
pixel_ratio.reasoning: DPR 3 (iPhone 14 Pro) means 9x more pixels than DPR 1. Cap at 2 — the visual difference between 2 and 3 is negligible but the performance cost is significant.
pixel_ratio.dynamic: On mobile, consider DPR 1.5 for challenging scenes

render_targets.description: Off-screen rendering for post-processing, GPGPU, and multi-pass effects
render_targets.creation: new THREE.WebGLRenderTarget(width, height, { type: THREE.FloatType, minFilter: THREE.LinearFilter })

ping_pong.description: Two render targets that alternate roles (read from A, write to B, swap)
ping_pong.use: GPGPU simulation, feedback effects, temporal filtering
ping_pong.implementation: let [read, write] = [rtA, rtB]; // ... render to write, read from read ... [read, write] = [write, read];

render_targets.disposal: renderTarget.dispose() — MUST be called when no longer needed

auto_clear.description: renderer.autoClear = false allows manual control of buffer clearing
usage: Manually call renderer.clear(color, depth, stencil) before first render pass
Render main scene (no clear — composites on top)
Render HUD layer (no clear — composites on top)
auto_clear.benefit: Allows multi-layer rendering without render-to-texture overhead

scissor_test.description: Render only within a specified rectangle of the viewport
scissor_test.use_case: Partial viewport updates, HUD rendering in corners only, split-screen
scissor_test.event_horizon_use: Could optimize HUD rendering to only update corner regions where data changes

logarithmic_depth_buffer.description: Replaces the standard depth buffer with a logarithmic distribution for better precision at large distances
logarithmic_depth_buffer.when_needed: Scenes with extreme depth range (near: 0.01, far: 100000) — like space scenes
logarithmic_depth_buffer.cost: Small fragment shader overhead (log operation per fragment)
logarithmic_depth_buffer.event_horizon_use: Essential — the scene spans from near-camera HUD elements to distant star fields

render_order.description: Control which objects render first without modifying the scene graph
render_order.usage: mesh.renderOrder = n — lower values render first
use_case: Force background to render before foreground
Ensure HUD renders last (on top of everything)

gpgpu_in_three_js.description: General-Purpose GPU computing using Three.js render targets as data textures

architecture.data_representation: Particle state (position, velocity, etc.) stored as RGBA pixels in a texture. Each pixel = one particle. 512×512 texture = 262,144 particles.
architecture.computation: A fullscreen quad with a simulation shader reads the previous state texture and writes the new state to a render target.
architecture.ping_pong: Two render targets alternate: read from one, write to other, swap each frame.
architecture.readback: The result texture is used directly as an attribute source in the particle rendering shader (no CPU readback).

data_texture_setup.description: Initialize particle data on CPU, upload as DataTexture
implementation: const data = new Float32Array(size * size * 4); // RGBA per particle
// Fill with initial positions (RGB) and mass/age (A)
const texture = new THREE.DataTexture(data, size, size, THREE.RGBAFormat, THREE.FloatType);

simulation_shader.description: Fragment shader that reads previous state and computes new state

pattern.vertex: Standard fullscreen quad vertex shader — just pass UVs
fragment: gl_FragColor = pos; // or vel, depending on which texture this pass writes

render_target_setup.description: Two render targets for ping-pong pattern
implementation: const rtA = new THREE.WebGLRenderTarget(size, size, rtOptions);
const rtB = new THREE.WebGLRenderTarget(size, size, rtOptions);
important_settings: NearestFilter — do NOT interpolate data textures (would corrupt values)
FloatType — need full float precision for position/velocity

connecting_to_particle_rendering.description: Use the GPGPU output texture as a vertex attribute source in the particle vertex shader
technique: Create InstancedBufferGeometry with a 'reference' attribute containing UVs that map to the data texture
In vertex shader: sample the position texture at the reference UV to get particle world position
This eliminates CPU readback — all computation stays on GPU
vertex_shader_pattern: attribute vec2 reference; // UV pointing to this particle's data in the texture

gpgpu_compute_utility.description: Three.js r165+ includes GPUComputationRenderer helper for GPGPU
usage: const gpuCompute = new GPUComputationRenderer(size, size, renderer);
gpgpu_compute_utility.advantages: Handles ping-pong, dependency management, and variable setup automatically
gpgpu_compute_utility.note: Internally uses the same FBO ping-pong technique described above

what_must_be_disposed.method: scene.traverse(child => { if (child.geometry) child.geometry.dispose(); if (child.material) { if (Array.isArray(child.material)) child.material.forEach(m => m.dispose()); else child.material.dispose(); } })

what_does_NOT_need_disposal: Lights (no GPU resources beyond shadow map, which is auto-managed)

material_texture_disposal.description: Materials may have multiple textures that each need disposal

renderer_info.description: renderer.info provides real-time GPU resource counts

properties.memory.geometries: Number of geometry objects in GPU memory
properties.memory.textures: Number of texture objects in GPU memory

renderer_info.monitoring: Log renderer.info.memory every few seconds. If geometries or textures increase over time without corresponding scene additions, you have a leak.

relevant_features: Improved BatchedMesh for automatic draw call batching
TSL (Three.js Shading Language) for cross-renderer shader authoring
Legacy color management removed — use SRGBColorSpace for textures

color_space_management.description: Three.js r152+ uses color management by default. Textures must specify their color space.
rules: Data textures (normal, roughness, GPGPU): texture.colorSpace = THREE.LinearSRGBColorSpace (default)
If colors look washed out: your texture colorSpace is probably wrong

before_launch: No geometry/texture leaks (monitor renderer.info.memory over 5 minutes)
All particle systems use instanced rendering
All post-processing at half resolution where acceptable
Transparent objects use additive blending where possible (no sort needed)
No object allocation in render loop (no 'new' keyword in animate())
No DOM reads in render loop (getBoundingClientRect, offsetWidth, etc.)
Performance tested on: Chrome, Firefox, Safari, mobile Chrome, mobile Safari
Device tier detection and quality scaling implemented

sources.title: How to Dispose of Objects in Three.js
